abstract.para: Resource constraints let you specify which nodes resources can run on, what order resources should start in, and which resources are dependent on one another.

important.title: Restrictions for constraints and primitive resources

listitem.para: Don't add colocation constraints to primitives in a resource group. Add a colocation constraint to the group instead.
Don't add any constraints to a primitive that has been cloned. Add constraints to the clone instead.

section.title: Scores and infinity
section.para: When defining constraints, you also need to deal with scores. Scores of all kinds are integral to how the cluster works. Practically everything from migrating a resource to deciding which resource to stop in a degraded cluster is achieved by manipulating scores. Scores are calculated on a per-resource basis and any node with a negative score for a resource cannot run that resource. After calculating the scores for a resource, the cluster then chooses the node with the highest score.

When defining resource constraints, you specify a score for each constraint. The score indicates the value you are assigning to this resource constraint. Constraints with higher scores are applied before those with lower scores. By creating additional location constraints with different scores for a given resource, you can specify an order for the nodes that a resource fails over to.

The score indicates the value you are assigning to this resource constraint. Positive values indicate the resource can run on the Node you specify in the next step. Negative values mean it should not run on that node. Constraints with higher scores are applied before those with lower scores.

The score determines the location relationship between the resources. Positive values indicate that the resources should run on the same node. Negative values indicate that the resources should not run on the same node. The score is combined with other factors to decide where to put the resource. You can set a score of either +inf or -inf, defining resources that must always or must never run on the same node. You can also use non-infinite scores. In that case the colocation is called advisory and the cluster may decide not to follow them in favor of not stopping other resources if there is a conflict.

section.title: Resource sets
section.para: As an alternative format for defining location, colocation or order constraints, you can use resource sets, where primitives are grouped together in one set. Previously this was possible either by defining a resource group (which could not always accurately express the design), or by defining each relationship as an individual constraint. The latter caused a constraint explosion as the number of resources and combinations grew. The configuration via resource sets is not necessarily less verbose, but is easier to understand and maintain.
Sets can be either ordered (sequential=true) or unordered (sequential=false). Furthermore, the require-all attribute can be used to switch between AND and OR logic.
Location constraints use curly {} brackets, order and colocation constraints use regular () brackets or sometimes square [] brackets. Why...?
